content.text: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is a reinforcement learning algorithm designed for fine-tuning language models on verifiable tasks such as mathematics, code generation, and logical reasoning. It eliminates the need for a separate critic (value) network by computing advantages from a group of sampled responses to the same prompt — a simple statistical normalisation that makes training cheaper and more stable than standard PPO.

attrs.alt: GRPO training loop: sample group, score, normalise advantages, PPO update

content.text: GRPO was introduced by DeepSeek AI as part of the DeepSeekMath and later DeepSeek-R1 training pipelines. Where Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) relies on a learned value function
to estimate advantages via Generalised Advantage Estimation (GAE), GRPO replaces the critic entirely by sampling
, scoring each with a reward model, and normalising those scores within the group.

The resulting group-relative advantages are statistically well-calibrated — their mean is exactly zero and standard deviation is one within each group — so they can be plugged directly into a PPO-style clipped surrogate without a separate critic update. This makes GRPO roughly half the memory footprint of PPO and significantly easier to tune.

Training large language models with PPO requires running four separate model copies simultaneously: the active policy, the reference (frozen SFT) model for KL computation, the reward model, and the value network. For a 70B parameter model, that means four copies sharing GPU memory — a considerable constraint in practice.

GRPO addresses this by removing the value network. The critic is replaced by statistical estimation: sample many responses, measure their rewards, and use the within-group z-score as the advantage signal. This is accurate when the group size
is large enough (typically 8–64) and the reward function is reliable — conditions that hold naturally for verifiable domains like maths (answer correct/incorrect) and code (tests pass/fail).

GRPO became widely known after it was used to train DeepSeek-R1-Zero — a reasoning model that learned chain-of-thought behaviour purely from RL, without supervised fine-tuning on reasoning traces — demonstrating that simple group-relative advantages are sufficient to produce sophisticated reasoning capabilities.
Eliminates one model copy (~25% memory saving per GPU), removes the critic loss term, and reduces hyperparameters.
Group normalisation is deterministic and requires no neural network training.
Directly compatible with verifiable reward functions.
Binary rewards (correct/incorrect) are particularly clean because half the group will typically be correct — creating a natural diversity of advantages.
completions forces the model to produce diverse outputs, which prevents premature convergence to a single template.
Scales to long chains of thought.
GRPO was used to train models producing multi-thousand-token reasoning traces without reward shaping beyond a terminal correctness signal.
Normalised advantages always lie in a predictable range (z-scores), avoiding the gradient magnitude instabilities that occur when raw rewards vary across batches.

GRPO works best when the reward signal is objective (mathematical correctness, code tests). Subjective tasks (summarisation, creative writing) need a learned RM, reintroducing its biases and instabilities.
completions per prompt multiplies inference cost by

High-variance advantages when G is small.
With only 2–4 samples per group, the group mean and standard deviation are noisy estimates. Effective GRPO typically needs
When all group members are either all correct or all incorrect (rare but possible), the normalised advantage is undefined (0/0); the group must be skipped.
Not suitable for tasks with very long episodes.
GRPO provides no per-step credit assignment. For episodes of 10,000+ tokens, the sparse terminal reward can take hundreds of thousands of steps to stabilise.

— it is a completion-level signal, not a per-token one.

Because the advantage is shared across all tokens of a completion, GRPO effectively treats the entire response as a single action taken from a multi-step categorical distribution. This is correct for verifiable tasks where the reward depends on the full response (e.g., mathematical answer at the end), but may under-specify credit assignment for tasks where intermediate steps matter independently.
The GRPO training loop requires three model instances: the active policy
(for KL computation), and the reward model RM. Unlike PPO, there is no value head and no critic update step.

GRPO is the right choice when the task has a verifiable, objective reward signal — mathematical correctness, code passing unit tests, logical entailment, structured output validity, or game winning/losing. In these settings, the reward is noise-free and binary or near-binary, making group normalisation particularly effective.
— helpfulness, creativity, tone — because the reward model introduces noise that amplifies within small groups.
— generating 16+ samples per prompt may be impractical for very large models; consider DPO or PPO with a critic.
The task requires dense per-step credit assignment
— for procedural tasks where intermediate steps need individual reward signals, PPO with a shaped reward and GAE is more appropriate.
A full GRPO implementation needs: a rollout function that generates
completions, a reward function, a normalise function, and the GRPO loss. The following shows a minimal but complete PyTorch implementation using the Hugging Face

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

def generate_group(model, tokenizer, prompt: str, G: int,
                   max_new_tokens: int = 512, temperature: float = 0.9,
                   device: str = "cuda") -> list[str]:
    """Sample G completions for a single prompt."""
    enc = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        # Replicate the prompt G times in a single batch
        input_ids = enc.input_ids.expand(G, -1)
        attention_mask = enc.attention_mask.expand(G, -1)
        outputs = model.generate(
            input_ids=input_ids,
            attention_mask=attention_mask,
            max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens,
            do_sample=True,
            temperature=temperature,
            pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id,
        )
    # Slice off the prompt prefix
    prompt_len = enc.input_ids.shape[1]
    completions = [
        tokenizer.decode(o[prompt_len:], skip_special_tokens=True)
        for o in outputs
    ]
    return completions


def group_relative_advantages(rewards: list[float], eps: float = 1e-8
                               ) -> list[float] | None:
    """Normalise rewards within the group. Returns None if all rewards equal."""
    import statistics
    if len(rewards) < 2:
        return None
    mu = statistics.mean(rewards)
    sigma = statistics.pstdev(rewards)
    if sigma < eps:
        return None          # degenerate group — skip
    return [(r - mu) / (sigma + eps) for r in rewards]


def sequence_log_prob(logits: torch.Tensor, input_ids: torch.Tensor,
                      response_mask: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Per-token log-probabilities summed over the masked response."""
    log_probs = F.log_softmax(logits[:, :-1, :], dim=-1)
    target_ids = input_ids[:, 1:].unsqueeze(-1)
    token_lp = log_probs.gather(2, target_ids).squeeze(-1)   # (B, T-1)
    mask = response_mask[:, 1:]                               # align
    # Return (B, T-1) masked log-probs for per-token ratio computation
    return token_lp * mask, mask

def grpo_loss(
    policy_logits_new: torch.Tensor,   # (G, L, V)  — current policy, requires_grad
    policy_logits_old: torch.Tensor,   # (G, L, V)  — frozen snapshot, no grad
    ref_logits: torch.Tensor,          # (G, L, V)  — frozen ref model
    input_ids: torch.Tensor,           # (G, L)
    response_mask: torch.Tensor,       # (G, L)  1 on response tokens
    advantages: torch.Tensor,          # (G,)    group-normalised
    clip_eps: float = 0.2,
    beta: float = 0.04,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    lp_new, mask = sequence_log_prob(policy_logits_new, input_ids, response_mask)
    lp_old, _    = sequence_log_prob(policy_logits_old, input_ids, response_mask)
    lp_ref, _    = sequence_log_prob(ref_logits,        input_ids, response_mask)

    ratio = (lp_new - lp_old.detach()).exp()           # (G, T)  importance ratio
    adv   = advantages.unsqueeze(-1)                   # (G, 1)  broadcast over T

    surr1 = ratio * adv
    surr2 = ratio.clamp(1 - clip_eps, 1 + clip_eps) * adv
    clip_loss = -torch.min(surr1, surr2)               # (G, T)

    kl = lp_old.detach() - lp_ref.detach()            # (G, T)  per-token KL approx
    per_token = clip_loss + beta * kl                  # (G, T)

    # Average over response tokens then over group members
    token_counts = mask.sum(-1).clamp(min=1)           # (G,)
    per_seq = (per_token * mask).sum(-1) / token_counts  # (G,)
    return per_seq.mean()

import torch, re
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

def extract_answer(text: str) -> str | None:
    """Pull \\boxed{...} or final line number from text."""
    match = re.search(r"\\boxed\{([^}]+)\}", text)
    if match:
        return match.group(1).strip()
    lines = [l.strip() for l in text.strip().splitlines() if l.strip()]
    return lines[-1] if lines else None

def math_reward(completion: str, ground_truth: str) -> float:
    pred = extract_answer(completion)
    return 1.0 if pred == ground_truth.strip() else 0.0

# --- Minimal training step ---
def grpo_step(model, ref_model, tokenizer, prompt: str, answer: str,
              G: int = 8, device: str = "cuda"):
    # 1. Rollout
    completions = generate_group(model, tokenizer, prompt, G,
                                  max_new_tokens=256, device=device)

    # 2. Score
    rewards = [math_reward(c, answer) for c in completions]

    # 3. Normalise
    advantages = group_relative_advantages(rewards)
    if advantages is None:
        return None   # all same reward — skip

    adv_tensor = torch.tensor(advantages, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)

    # 4. Tokenize group
    full_texts = [prompt + c for c in completions]
    enc = tokenizer(full_texts, return_tensors="pt", padding=True,
                    truncation=True, max_length=1024).to(device)
    prompt_len = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids.shape[1]
    response_mask = torch.zeros_like(enc.input_ids, dtype=torch.float32)
    response_mask[:, prompt_len:] = (enc.input_ids[:, prompt_len:] != tokenizer.pad_token_id).float()

    # 5. Old and ref logits (no grad)
    with torch.no_grad():
        old_logits = model(**enc).logits
        ref_logits = ref_model(**enc).logits

    # 6. Current logits (with grad)
    new_logits = model(**enc).logits

    loss = grpo_loss(new_logits, old_logits, ref_logits,
                     enc.input_ids, response_mask, adv_tensor)
    return loss, sum(rewards) / len(rewards)

from torch.optim import AdamW
from torch.optim.lr_scheduler import CosineAnnealingLR

def grpo_train(
    model,
    ref_model,
    tokenizer,
    dataset,           # list of {"prompt": str, "answer": str}
    epochs: int = 3,
    lr: float = 5e-7,
    G: int = 16,
    batch_size: int = 8,
    clip_eps: float = 0.2,
    beta: float = 0.04,
    device: str = "cuda",
):
    ref_model.eval()
    for p in ref_model.parameters():
        p.requires_grad_(False)

    optimizer = AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=lr, weight_decay=0.01)
    scheduler = CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=epochs * len(dataset) // batch_size)

    for epoch in range(epochs):
        import random
        random.shuffle(dataset)
        total_loss, total_reward, skipped = 0.0, 0.0, 0

        for i in range(0, len(dataset), batch_size):
            batch = dataset[i:i + batch_size]
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            batch_loss = torch.tensor(0.0, device=device, requires_grad=True)
            batch_count = 0

            for item in batch:
                result = grpo_step(model, ref_model, tokenizer,
                                   item["prompt"], item["answer"],
                                   G=G, device=device)
                if result is None:
                    skipped += 1
                    continue
                loss, avg_reward = result
                batch_loss = batch_loss + loss
                total_reward += avg_reward
                batch_count += 1

            if batch_count > 0:
                (batch_loss / batch_count).backward()
                torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
                optimizer.step()
                scheduler.step()
                total_loss += batch_loss.item()

        n = len(dataset) - skipped
        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}: loss={total_loss/max(n,1):.4f}, "
              f"reward={total_reward/max(n,1):.3f}, skipped={skipped}")
DeepSeek-R1-Zero used GRPO with a verifiable math reward to produce chain-of-thought reasoning from a base language model — without any supervised reasoning data. The setup:
DeepSeek-V3-Base (671B MoE, frozen expert weights, only attention/gate layers trained).
tags) plus accuracy reward (correct final answer).
Curated math competition problems (AMC, AIME, MATH dataset).
— low, allowing significant divergence from reference during reasoning acquisition.

The key observation was that GRPO alone was sufficient to produce spontaneous reasoning behaviours — long thinking traces, self-correction, and backtracking — that were not present in the base model. This validated that group-relative advantages, when combined with a precise verifiable reward, can elicit structural reasoning without explicit imitation of reasoning data.

SYSTEM_PROMPT = (
    "A conversation between User and Assistant. The assistant thinks step by step "    "inside <think>...</think> tags and gives the final answer inside "    "<answer>...</answer> tags."
)

def format_reward(completion: str) -> float:
    """Reward correct format: thinking block + answer block present."""
    has_think  = bool(re.search(r"<think>.*?</think>", completion, re.DOTALL))
    has_answer = bool(re.search(r"<answer>.*?</answer>", completion, re.DOTALL))
    return 0.5 * float(has_think) + 0.5 * float(has_answer)

def combined_reward(completion: str, ground_truth: str) -> float:
    """Format (0.2 weight) + accuracy (0.8 weight) combined reward."""
    fmt   = format_reward(completion)
    # Extract content of <answer> tag
    m = re.search(r"<answer>(.*?)</answer>", completion, re.DOTALL)
    pred  = m.group(1).strip() if m else ""
    corr  = 1.0 if pred == ground_truth.strip() else 0.0
    return 0.2 * fmt + 0.8 * corr
to be a constant. If you recompute it inside the computation graph, gradients flow through the denominator and the loss can trivially be minimised by reducing the denominator.
completions receive identical rewards, the normalised advantage is 0/0. These groups must be silently dropped — they contribute zero useful signal and may produce NaN gradients.
With very small groups, the normalisation statistics are unreliable.
Applying GRPO to subjective tasks without a strong RM.
If the reward model has high noise, group normalisation amplifies that noise into the advantage signal. Only use GRPO with objective or high-confidence reward functions.
Forgetting to zero out prompt log-probs in the response mask.
The response mask must be 0 for all prompt tokens. Accidentally including prompt tokens in the loss inflates the gradient magnitude and trains the model to repeat the prompt.
Running too many PPO epochs on stale rollouts.

GRPO rollouts are expensive (G completions per prompt). Re-using them for many PPO epochs causes the importance ratio to drift far from 1, invalidating the clipping bound. Limit to 1–4 epochs per rollout batch.
Set G based on expected reward variance.
is often sufficient. For continuous rewards, increase to 16–32.
Track the fraction of groups skipped due to
. A high skip rate (>30%) suggests the model has saturated the reward or the prompts are too easy.
for the first few thousand steps to allow the model to find reward-gaining directions freely, then linearly increase to the target value.
Generating G completions per prompt is the training bottleneck. Tensor-parallel or pipeline-parallel inference can increase throughput 4–8× on multi-GPU systems.
Monitor clip fraction and ratio statistics.
If the mean importance ratio diverges beyond [0.8, 1.2], reduce learning rate or decrease rollout reuse epochs.
Checkpoint by reward, not by loss.
The loss decreases monotonically as the model overfits to the rollout batch, but reward on held-out prompts may peak earlier.
The dominant cost in GRPO training is rollout generation: producing
completions for each prompt. For a model of size
per prompt — the same as running
independent generation jobs. The gradient update step is comparatively cheap.
Running generation with vLLM's continuous batching and PagedAttention can achieve 5–10× higher throughput than naive
Decouple rollout generation (on inference GPUs) from gradient updates (on training GPUs). The rollout servers stream completed episodes to a replay buffer while the training loop consumes them.
Keep the policy in BF16; the value-free design means no stability risk from mixed-precision critic training.
During the update step, use gradient checkpointing on the transformer blocks to reduce activation memory by ~30–40%.
16 is most common; larger G improves advantage accuracy
Lower for reasoning tasks; higher for preference alignment
Much lower than SFT; policy starts near optimal
Reasoning tasks need longer; math answers shorter
GRPO support has been added to several open-source training frameworks following the DeepSeek-R1 release.
class in TRL provides a high-level interface. Requires a reward function callback; integrates with PEFT for LoRA-based GRPO.
Production-grade library supporting GRPO with vLLM-backed rollout generation, Ray-based distributed training, and multi-reward ensembles.
Alibaba's distributed RL library designed for large-scale GRPO; supports hybrid parallelism across hundreds of GPUs.
Provides GRPO support with a configuration-file-based interface for less code-intensive experiments.

# TRL GRPOTrainer example
from trl import GRPOConfig, GRPOTrainer
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

def reward_fn(completions: list[str], **kwargs) -> list[float]:
    """TRL reward functions receive a list of completions, return list of floats."""
    answers = kwargs.get("answers", [])
    return [
        1.0 if extract_answer(c) == a else 0.0
        for c, a in zip(completions, answers)
    ]

training_args = GRPOConfig(
    output_dir="grpo-math",
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=2,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
    num_generations=16,             # G
    max_new_tokens=512,
    temperature=0.9,
    beta=0.04,
    clip_eps=0.2,
    learning_rate=5e-7,
    logging_steps=10,
    bf16=True,
)

trainer = GRPOTrainer(
    model=AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("deepseek-ai/deepseek-math-7b-base"),
    tokenizer=AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("deepseek-ai/deepseek-math-7b-base"),
    reward_funcs=[reward_fn],
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=train_dataset,
)
trainer.train()

The key differentiator between GRPO and PPO is the absence of a critic: GRPO's advantage signal is purely statistical (group rank) while PPO's is model-based (learned value function). For verifiable tasks, group rank is a sufficient and noise-free advantage signal. For tasks with dense per-step rewards or very sparse terminal signals over long episodes, PPO's critic adds value that GRPO cannot provide.

GRPO with verifiable rewards is more resistant to reward hacking than GRPO with a learned reward model, because the ground-truth reward function cannot be exploited by generating superficially plausible outputs. However, format rewards (structured tagging requirements) can be exploited — the model may learn to include mandatory tags while the actual content is low quality.
Mitigations: weight accuracy reward heavily relative to format reward; evaluate on out-of-distribution problems throughout training; impose response length penalties to prevent padding attacks.

If the KL coefficient is set too low, GRPO can produce a policy that drifts far from the reference model and generates incoherent text outside the training distribution. Monitor KL divergence from the reference model per step; if it exceeds 2–3 nats, pause training and reduce learning rate.

Group relative advantages are computed per-prompt. Adversarially crafted prompts that cause all group members to fail (reward=0) or all to succeed (reward=1) will be filtered out (σ_G=0) but waste compute. In production, maintain a curriculum of difficulty-balanced prompts to ensure group diversity.
GRPO replaces the PPO critic with group-relative advantage normalisation — completions within a group are ranked by reward, and the z-score of each reward becomes the advantage signal.
where the mean and std are computed across
GRPO eliminates the value network, reducing training memory by roughly one model copy and removing the need to tune critic hyperparameters.
It is most effective for verifiable tasks (math, code, logic) with objective reward signals; subjective tasks with noisy RMs are better served by PPO or offline methods.
Groups where all rewards are identical must be skipped — they produce zero-gradient and undefined normalisation.
Practical group size is typically G=8–16; smaller groups produce noisy advantages, larger groups increase rollout compute proportionally.
DeepSeek-R1-Zero demonstrated that GRPO with a verifiable reward alone can elicit spontaneous chain-of-thought reasoning without any supervised reasoning data.
and OpenRLHF provide production-grade implementations with vLLM-backed rollout generation for large-scale training.
